summary: I come from Changde, a city of nearly six million people in the Hunan province of the People's Republic of China. It is the place where I was born before being adopted and brought to the United States. Although I have no memories from there, learning about Changde helped me understand my origins and sparked my desire to reconnect with my birth culture. My trips back to China, including Changde, were pivotal in my journey of self-discovery.

Hunan is the province in the People's Republic of China where Changde, my birth city, is located. Knowing that I come from Hunan gave me a geographical anchor as I searched for my roots after being adopted by an American family. My connection to Hunan was distant at first, but it became more meaningful as I studied Mandarin and traveled back to China. It represents the starting point of my identity as a queer transracial adoptee.

The People's Republic of China is my birth country, where I was born and adopted from as an infant. Although I was raised in the United States, I have always felt a complex connection to China, especially after returning there in 2013 and again during college. These visits were emotional and challenging, as I felt foreign despite my origins. Studying Mandarin and learning about Chinese culture have been important parts of reclaiming my identity as one of China's lost daughters.

I have lived most of my life in the United States since being adopted from China as a baby. My family settled first in Florida, then Ohio, and finally Middleton, Wisconsin, where I spent twelve years growing up. The U.S. is where I began questioning my identity, joined LGBTQ+ groups, and found communities that helped me embrace who I am. It is also where I attended the University of Minnesota and became an activist and storyteller.

Florida was the first place my adoptive family and I lived after returning from China in 1997. It was there that I rejoined my older brother and became part of a family of four. Although I was young, Florida was the starting point of my life in America and the beginning of my journey as a transracial adoptee. My parents were open about my adoption from the very beginning, which helped me understand my identity as I grew.

After living in Florida, my family moved to Ohio before eventually settling in Middleton, Wisconsin. Ohio was part of the transition period in my childhood as my family grew and I began to explore who I was. Although I don't recall many specific events there, it was a step in my family's journey across the United States. This move set the stage for my later years in Wisconsin where I became more active in LGBTQ+ communities.

Middleton, Wisconsin, is where I spent twelve formative years of my life, including middle and high school. It was in Middleton that I began questioning my identity and joined my school's Gay–straight alliance. This community helped me engage in activism and attend the Leadership Training Institute in 2014. Middleton was the backdrop for much of my self-discovery and the place where I found my voice as a queer Asian adoptee.

I joined my high school's Gay–straight alliance shortly after starting high school in Middleton, Wisconsin. This group was my first real connection to the LGBTQ+ community and a place where I could explore my identity safely. Through the GSA, I participated in important events like Day of Silence and Transgender Day of Remembrance, which deepened my commitment to activism. The alliance was instrumental in helping me find support and courage during a confusing time.

Day of Silence was one of the key events I participated in through my school's Gay–straight alliance. It is a day dedicated to raising awareness about the silencing and erasure of LGBTQ+ people. Taking part in this event during high school helped me understand the importance of visibility and solidarity. It was part of my early activist work that shaped my identity and commitment to fighting for queer rights.

Transgender Day of Remembrance was another significant event I engaged with through my school's Gay–straight alliance. This day honors the memory of transgender people lost to violence and discrimination. Participating in this event helped me connect with the struggles of the transgender community and reinforced my own journey as a queer trans person. It was a powerful reminder of why activism and visibility matter.

The Leadership Training Institute, or LTI, was a camp for high school LGBTQ+ activists in Wisconsin that I attended in August 2014. This experience was monumental for me because it gave me the courage and knowledge to define my queer identity openly. At LTI, I also participated in the Queer Asian Caucus, which was my first time confronting the racism I had experienced. LTI was a turning point that helped me embrace who I am and find a supportive community.

The Queer Asian Caucus was a race-based discussion group at the Leadership Training Institute where I first found a community of queer Asians. It was the first time I could openly talk about the racism I had experienced alongside my queer identity. This caucus was both healing and raw, and it helped me feel less alone by connecting me with people who shared my struggles and identities. The relationships I formed there became some of the most important in my life.

During my senior year of high school, I joined Proud Theater, a queer theater group for LGBTQ+ youth and allies. Proud Theater gave me the opportunity to tell my own story in my own words, which was incredibly empowering and healing. It allowed me to share my experiences and educate others about injustice through performance. Being part of Proud Theater strengthened my voice as an activist and storyteller.

I am currently a student at the University of Minnesota Twin Cities, studying political science and Mandarin Chinese. The university provided me with opportunities to return to China after my freshman year through a study program, which deepened my understanding of my cultural roots. My time at the University of Minnesota has been crucial in shaping my identity and academic goals, helping me live an authentic and honest life.
